chapter_context.summary: Pronounces a woe on Ariel (Jerusalem), describing the sudden visitation of enemies, the spiritual blindness of the people, and the future transformation of Lebanon.

outline.text: Ariel Besieged, Brought Low, and Suddenly Delivered
outline.explanation: This section focuses on Ariel Besieged, Brought Low, and Suddenly Delivered (29:1-8), highlighting its theological significance and showing how it develops the central message of Isaiah.

outline.text: Deep Sleep of Blind Prophets; Rebuking Lips-only Worship
outline.explanation: This section focuses on Deep Sleep of Blind Prophets; Rebuking Lips-only Worship (29:9-16), highlighting its theological significance and showing how it develops the central message of Isaiah.

outline.text: Lebanon Turned into a Fruitful Field; Deaf and Blind Hearing
outline.explanation: This section focuses on Lebanon Turned into a Fruitful Field; Deaf and Blind Hearing (29:17-24), highlighting its theological significance and showing how it develops the central message of Isaiah.

key_themes.title: Prophecy of Judgment
key_themes.description: This chapter explores key facets of prophecy of judgment, revealing how God's holy character directs both judgment and redemption.

key_themes.title: The Holy One of Israel
key_themes.description: Isaiah's signature title for God, highlighting His absolute transcendent holiness, moral purity, and unique relationship with His people.

devotional.question: Reflecting on the warnings and promises of Chapter 29, how does God's double character of absolute holiness and deep comfort speak to your life today?
How does this chapter encourage you to trust in God's promises of ultimate renewal and restoration in times of spiritual or physical exile?

cultural_historical.title: The Role of the Prophet in Israel
cultural_historical.text: Prophets in the Hebrew Bible were not just fortune-tellers, but 'covenant enforcement agents'—called to confront sin, demand social justice, and bring hope of future restoration.
